title: Urashima Taro: The Fisherman and the Sea Kingdom
summary: A kind fisherman is rewarded with a visit to the underwater Dragon Palace, only to return to a world where centuries have passed in a heartbeat.
full_text:
Long ago, in the province of Tango, in the little fishing village of Mizu-no-ye, there lived a young fisherman named Urashima Taro. He was kind-hearted and skilled, able to catch more bonito and bream in a day than his companions could in a week. One afternoon, as he walked along the shore, he found a group of children tormenting a small turtle. They poked it with sticks and threw stones, laughing as the creature struggled toward the sea. "Stop!" Urashima cried, and he shooed the children away. He carried the turtle gently to the water's edge and released it.

The next day, as he fished from his boat, a great turtle surfaced beside him. "Urashima Taro," it said, "I am the turtle you saved. In gratitude, let me take you to the Palace of the Dragon King beneath the sea." Urashima, astonished, climbed onto the turtle's back. Down they dove, through waters of deepening blue, past coral reefs and schools of glittering fish, until they reached a palace of such beauty that Urashima could not speak. Its walls were of pearl, its roof of coral, its gardens of seaweed that swayed like dancers in an underwater breeze.

The princess Otohime came forth to greet him. She was more beautiful than any creature of the upper world, robed in silks that shimmered like sea foam. "I was the turtle you saved," she said. "Will you stay here with me, in the land of eternal youth? Marry me, and live forever without age or pain." Urashima was enchanted. Days passed in feasting and dancing. The fishes performed ballets, the dragons sang, and every pleasure imaginable was his. He forgot the world above, forgot his mother, forgot his village.

But at last, a shadow crossed his heart. "I must return home," he said. "My parents will be worried." Otohime wept, but she did not stop him. She gave him a small lacquer box tied with a silk cord. "This is the tamatebako," she said. "Never open it. As long as you keep it closed, you carry my love." Urashima returned to the surface, but as he stepped onto the beach, he found the village unrecognizable. The houses were different, the people were strangers, and the names of his parents were only distant memories in old books. He discovered that while he had spent only a few years in the Dragon Palace, three hundred years had passed on land. Everyone he had ever known was long dead.

Overwhelmed by grief and loneliness, Urashima forgot the princess's warning. In a moment of desperation, he untied the silk cord and opened the tamatebako. A plume of white smoke billowed out, and as it touched his skin, the three centuries of lost time caught up with him in a single heartbeat. His hair turned white, his skin wrinkled, and his strength vanished. In an instant, he became an old man, weathered by the weight of time. He sank to the sand, looking out at the ocean, realizing that the price of a momentary paradise was the loss of everything he had ever truly loved.
moral: Time is the most precious of all treasures; to ignore it is to lose the very essence of human connection.

title: Uksáhkká: Guardian of the Door
summary: Uksáhkká (Door Woman) is one of the daughters of Máttaráhkká, living under the door of the goahti. She protects people entering and leaving the dwelling, receives the newborn child into the world during birth, and protects children as they learn to walk. Drinks were poured under the door as offerings. Christian influence equated her with Virgin Mary. She was known only among Scandinavian Sámi.
full_text: Uksáhkká dwells beneath the threshold of every goahti, in the invisible space between inside and outside. Her name means 'Door Woman,' and her domain is the boundary of the home. Whenever someone steps through the door, Uksáhkká watches over them, protecting them from harm as they pass from the safety of the hearth to the dangers of the wilderness. Her most important task comes during birth. When the child emerges from the mother, Sáráhkká passes it to Uksáhkká, who receives it into the world. She is the first divine being the newborn meets, the one who welcomes them to the middle world. As the child grows and learns to walk, Uksáhkká steadies their steps, preventing falls and keeping them from wandering too far. Offerings were made to her by pouring drinks under the door. Christian missionaries later told the Sámi that Uksáhkká was actually the Virgin Mary, but the old women knew the difference. Uksáhkká was known only among the Scandinavian Sámi of Norway and Sweden, not in Finland or Russia, her worship tied to the specific shape of the goahti door.
moral: The threshold between worlds is sacred, guarded by those who welcome and protect.

title: Unhcegila the Serpent Monster
summary: Unhcegila was a gigantic serpent monster who wreaked havoc upon the land, devouring anyone who stumbled upon her. A red crystal in the seventh spot on her head functioned as her heart and granted its bearer great power. In one version, a warrior from the Bear Clan, guided by a Weasel spirit, allowed himself to be devoured then cut his way out, freeing other victims. In another, two brothers—one blind—killed her with arrows from a medicine woman. A third version tells of Unhcegila emerging from primordial waters, only to be struck down by Wakinyan the Thunderbird whose lightning shattered her heart, scattering her bones across the Badlands.
source: Wikipedia / Native American Mythology A to Z

title: Uktena: The Horned Serpent (Cherokee)
summary: Uktena is a massive horned serpent covered in shining scales, with a single blazing crystal in its forehead that grants supernatural vision. The first Uktena was once a man who attempted to kill the sun. He failed and was transformed into the monstrous serpent as punishment. Uktena dwells in deep rivers, lakes, and under mountains. Its gaze alone can paralyze or kill. Only the bravest medicine men and heroes dare to hunt Uktena, seeking the Ulûñsû'tî, the diamond-like crystal from its forehead, which grants great power to whoever possesses it. The most famous story involves a hunter who stalked an Uktena to its lair and, after a great battle, retrieved the crystal. The stone brought power but also danger, and was eventually hidden away in a sacred place.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org Uktena

title: Unkulunkulu: The First Man Who Broke from the Reeds
summary: In the beginning there was only darkness and Uthlanga, the primordial reed bed. The god Umvelinqangi created a seed that grew into a massive reed, and at the end of one reed grew a man named Unkulunkulu, the 'old old one.' He grew so large that he snapped off from the reed and walked across the land, where he found men and women sprouting from other reeds. He picked them and they became the first ancestors of all nations. He gathered fish and flung them into rivers, harvested birds and antelope, and plucked cattle for humans. He threw a ball of fire and a glowing stone into the sky, creating the Sun and Moon. He also sent the chameleon with the message that men should not die, but the slow chameleon was beaten by a fast lizard carrying the opposite message, and so death entered the world.

title: Uekera: The Tree of Knowledge
summary: In Kiribati legend, Uekera is a sacred tree that reaches to the heavens, known as the tree of knowledge. It was planted in Buariki village in North Tarawa by the goddess Nei Tekanuea. According to tradition, Uekera's roots extend deep into the earth while its branches reach into the sky world, making it a bridge between the human and divine realms. The tree is said to possess great wisdom and those who approach it with respect can gain knowledge and insight. Uekera inspired the name of Kiribati's weekly newspaper, Te Uekera. The tree symbolizes the connection between the people of Kiribati, their land, and their ancestors, representing the transmission of cultural knowledge from generation to generation through oral tradition.

title: Umar Marvi — The Faithful Woman of Sindh
summary: Marvi was a beautiful peasant woman from the Thar Desert village of Malir in Sindh. The local ruler, Umar Soomro, became obsessed with her and had her kidnapped and brought to his palace. He offered her wealth, jewels, and queenship if she would marry him, but Marvi refused steadfastly, remaining faithful to her betrothed, a simple farmer. She endured years of captivity and torture, never wavering in her loyalty. Eventually, Umar Soomro was so impressed by her unwavering virtue that he released her. Marvi returned to her village and married her true love. The story, immortalized in Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai's Shah Jo Risalo, is one of the seven tragic romances of Sindhi folklore and is celebrated as a symbol of truth, loyalty, and the unbreakable connection to one's homeland.
source: Shah Jo Risalo by Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai (18th century), Sindhi folklore
moral: Love transcends death but society's cruelty destroys it

title: Udea and Her Seven Brothers
summary: A Libyan fairy tale collected by Hans Stumme in Tripoli. A woman with seven sons gives birth to a daughter, Udea. The seven brothers are driven away from home by a rival female spirit and set out into the world. The mother promises them a sign when Udea is born: a red flag for a daughter, a white one for a son. The rival spirit arranges for a white flag to be raised instead of red, tricking the brothers into thinking they have another brother. Years later, Udea grows up, learns of her brothers, and sets out to find them. She travels through dangerous territory and eventually locates them, but her sister-in-law becomes jealous and sets impossible tasks for her, including fetching a magical bird and finding a singing tree. With supernatural help, Udea completes each task. Eventually, the truth is revealed, the jealous sister-in-law is punished, and Udea is reunited with her family. The tale includes themes of sibling loyalty, jealousy, and the triumph of perseverance over supernatural obstacles.
source: Wikipedia - Udea and Her Seven Brothers; snip Lang - The Grey Fairy Book
